reasoning: Both models failed to actually review any files. They both skipped the PKGBUILD with the message 'Skipping binary file: PKGBUILD', which is a tooling misclassification — a PKGBUILD is a plain-text shell script and should never be treated as a binary file. As a result, zero substantive analysis was performed, and no verdict on safety can be reached. The package may be safe or unsafe; we simply have no evidence either way.
disagreement_analysis: There is no disagreement on substance — both models produced identical 'skipped' results due to the same tooling/file-classification bug. The trigger note ('shallow, reviewed 0 files') reflects this shared failure rather than a difference in judgment.
coverage_issues: PKGBUILD was never reviewed — the single most important file for any AUR package audit was skipped by both models.
No .install hooks, source files, or supplementary scripts were enumerated or reviewed; coverage was effectively zero.
The binary-file detection heuristic falsely classified a plain-text PKGBUILD as binary, aborting the entire audit.
false_negatives: Any potential risks in PKGBUILD (download sources, checksums, build/package/prepare functions, .install scripts) are entirely unassessed because the file was skipped — not a missed finding per se, but a complete absence of analysis.
re_audit_focus: PKGBUILD — force text-mode reading and review source URLs, sha sums, and all build/package/prepare/check functions
Verify whether sources are official NVIDIA binary blobs vs. third-party mirrors and that checksums pin to expected versions
learnings: Fix the binary-file detection heuristic: PKGBUILD (and other known shell-script filenames like .install, .sh) must always be force-treated as text and never skipped as binary.
Add a hard guard: if the PKGBUILD itself cannot be read/reviewed, the audit should escalate to a fatal error requiring manual review rather than silently emitting a benign-looking 'skipped' result.
File-type detection should use an allowlist of known package filenames (PKGBUILD, *.install, *.sh, *.hook) that bypass binary sniffing entirely.
Reporting could be misleading: 'No issues found.' appears in output despite zero files reviewed — the summary text should not imply safety when nothing was analyzed.

Running multiple models is wasted effort when a shared tooling bug short-circuits all of them identically; consider a pre-flight sanity check that the PKGBUILD is parseable before invoking model audits.
